hints.Nominative: While the form is the same, Augustine is addressing God directly — this is vocative
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'Deum' — 'Deus' here is vocative of direct address
hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'Dei' — 'Deus' is vocative, addressing God

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'Deus' with -us ending is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have varied endings — 'Deus, Dei' is standard 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns also end in -us but have different genitive — 'Deus, Dei' is 2nd declension

hints.to be new: 'to be new' is not a standard Latin verb — 'noverim' comes from 'noscere' (to know)
hints.to harm: 'to harm' is 'nocere' — do not confuse with 'noscere'
hints.to renew: 'to renew' is 'renovare' — 'noverim' is from 'noscere' (to come to know)

hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'noscam' — 'noverim' with perfect stem 'nov-' + -erim is perfect subjunctive
hints.Perfect indicative: Perfect indicative would be 'novi' — 'noverim' with the -erim ending is subjunctive
hints.Future perfect indicative: Future perfect would be 'novero' — 'noverim' is perfect subjunctive expressing a wish

hints.noceo, nocere, nocui, nocitum: These are the parts of 'nocere' (to harm) — 'noverim' comes from 'noscere'
hints.novus, nova, novum (adj.): This is an adjective meaning 'new' — 'noverim' is a verb form from 'noscere'
hints.nolo, nolle, nolui: These are the parts of 'nolle' (to be unwilling) — not related to 'noscere'

hints.The same forever: Close but imprecise — 'semper' means 'always' and 'idem' means 'the same'
hints.Always himself: 'himself' would be 'se' or 'ipse' — 'idem' specifically means 'the same'
hints.The eternal one: 'The eternal one' would be 'aeternus' — 'idem' means 'the same' (unchanging)

steps.prompt: The repeated 'noverim ... noverim' structure expresses what kind of wish?
steps.target: An optative wish — 'may I know myself, may I know you'
choices: An optative wish — 'may I know myself, may I know you'

hints.A command to others: Commands use the imperative — the subjunctive 'noverim' expresses a personal wish
hints.A past contrary-to-fact condition: Contrary-to-fact uses pluperfect subjunctive — perfect subjunctive here expresses an attainable wish
hints.A statement of certainty: Certainty uses the indicative — the subjunctive signals wish or desire

steps.prompt: What is the theological pairing Augustine establishes?
steps.target: Self-knowledge and knowledge of God are inseparable
choices: Self-knowledge and knowledge of God are inseparable
Human knowledge is superior to divine

hints.God is unknowable: Augustine prays 'noverim te' — he believes knowing God is possible
hints.Human knowledge is superior to divine: Augustine places both on equal footing: 'noverim me, noverim te'
hints.Knowledge is impossible without faith: While Augustine values faith, this prayer emphasizes the twin pursuit of self-knowledge and knowledge of God

translation: God, ever the same, may I know myself, may I know you.
